---
title: "Contrasts"
subtitle: "Create named hypotheses from design-matrix columns."
jupyter: python3
execute:
  freeze: auto
---

## A 2 x 2 Event Design

The original R vignette built contrasts for a factorial event model. In
Python, start by making the factorial cells explicit in the event table.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import fmrimod as fm
from fmrimod.design_colmap import design_colmap

design = pd.DataFrame({
    "category": ["face", "face", "scene", "scene"] * 3,
    "attention": ["attend", "ignore", "attend", "ignore"] * 3,
    "onset": np.arange(0, 96, 8),
})

sf = fm.SamplingFrame(blocklens=120, TR=1.0)
model = fm.event_model(
    'onset ~ hrf(category, attention, basis="spmg1")',
    data=design,
    sampling_frame=sf,
)

design_colmap(model)[["col", "name", "condition"]]
```

## Pair and Unit Contrasts

A pair contrast is just a named vector in design-column order. This explicit
representation is often the clearest way to audit the hypothesis before
fitting a model.

```{python}
columns = model.column_names

face_cols = [i for i, name in enumerate(columns) if "face" in name]
scene_cols = [i for i, name in enumerate(columns) if "scene" in name]

face_vs_scene = np.zeros(len(columns))
face_vs_scene[face_cols] = 1 / len(face_cols)
face_vs_scene[scene_cols] = -1 / len(scene_cols)

pd.DataFrame({"column": columns, "weight": face_vs_scene})
```

A unit-style contrast compares a group of columns to the implicit baseline.

```{python}
face_vs_baseline = np.zeros(len(columns))
face_vs_baseline[face_cols] = 1 / len(face_cols)

pd.DataFrame({"column": columns, "weight": face_vs_baseline})
```

## Main Effects and Interactions

`fmrimod` also exposes fast factorial contrast generators for balanced cell
layouts.

```{python}
cells = pd.DataFrame({
    "category": pd.Categorical(["face", "scene"]),
    "attention": pd.Categorical(["attend", "ignore"]),
})

category_main = fm.generate_main_effect_contrast(cells, "category")
interaction = fm.generate_interaction_contrast(cells, ["category", "attention"])

category_main.shape, interaction.shape
```

```{python}
pd.DataFrame(
    np.column_stack([category_main, interaction]),
    columns=["category_main", "category_attention_interaction"],
)
```

## Apply a Contrast After Fitting

The GLM helper returns coefficient, standard error, and t-statistic matrices.
A contrast estimate can be formed by multiplying the fitted beta matrix by the
contrast vector.

```{python}
rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
X = np.asarray(model.design_matrix)
true_beta = np.linspace(0.4, 1.0, X.shape[1])[:, None]
Y = X @ true_beta + rng.normal(scale=0.05, size=(X.shape[0], 4))

fit = fm.fmri_ols_fit(Y, X)
contrast_estimate = face_vs_scene @ fit["beta"]
contrast_estimate.round(3)
```

## Next

- [Linear model](linear-model.qmd)
- [Design matrices](design-matrices.qmd)
